---
title: "Seaborn for Statistical Data Visualization"
author: "N.L.N.Kumarage (AS2022469)"
format:
  html:
    theme: cosmo
    toc: true
    toc-depth: 2
    code-fold: false
    code-tools: false
    smooth-scroll: true
execute:
  warning: false
  message: false
---

# Introduction

Seaborn is a Python library built on top of Matplotlib used for **statistical data visualization**. It is mainly used for exploring data and understanding patterns.

It helps to: - Understand distributions\
- Compare categories\
- Study relationships\
- Visualize correlations

------------------------------------------------------------------------

# Installation Guide

Seaborn must be installed before use.

## Using pip

Use pip when you are working with: - Standard Python installation - VS Code / PyCharm / Jupyter (non-Anaconda environments)

Command: pip install seaborn

------------------------------------------------------------------------

## Using conda

Use conda when you are working with: - Anaconda / Miniconda - Data science environments with pre-installed packages

Command: conda install seaborn

------------------------------------------------------------------------

# Dataset: Tips

We use the built-in Seaborn dataset called **tips**, which contains restaurant billing information.

```{python}
import seaborn as sns
import matplotlib.pyplot as plt
import numpy as np

sns.set_theme(style="whitegrid")

tips = sns.load_dataset("tips")
tips.head()
```

### Dataset Overview

-   total_bill → total bill amount\
-   tip → tip given\
-   sex → gender of customer\
-   smoker → smoking status\
-   day → day of visit\
-   time → lunch or dinner\
-   size → number of people

------------------------------------------------------------------------

# 1. Distribution Plots

These plots show how values are distributed.

## Histogram

```{python}
sns.histplot(data=tips, x="total_bill", bins=20)
plt.title("Distribution of Total Bill")
plt.show()
```

### Explanation

Shows how frequently different bill values occur.

------------------------------------------------------------------------

## Histogram with KDE

```{python}
sns.histplot(data=tips, x="total_bill", kde=True)
plt.title("Histogram with KDE")
plt.show()
```

### Explanation

Combines frequency distribution with a smooth density curve.

------------------------------------------------------------------------

## KDE Plot

```{python}
sns.kdeplot(data=tips, x="total_bill", fill=True)
plt.title("KDE Plot")
plt.show()
```

### Explanation

Shows the probability density of the data.

------------------------------------------------------------------------

## Joint Plot

```{python}
sns.jointplot(data=tips, x="total_bill", y="tip", kind="scatter")
```

### Explanation

Shows relationship between two variables along with their individual distributions.

------------------------------------------------------------------------

## Pair Plot

```{python}
sns.pairplot(tips)
```

### Explanation

Shows all pairwise relationships between numerical variables.

------------------------------------------------------------------------

# 2. Categorical Data Plots

Used to compare values across groups.

------------------------------------------------------------------------

## Box Plot

```{python}
sns.boxplot(data=tips, x="day", y="total_bill")
plt.title("Box Plot by Day")
plt.show()
```

### Explanation

Shows median, quartiles, and outliers.

------------------------------------------------------------------------

## Violin Plot

```{python}
sns.violinplot(data=tips, x="day", y="total_bill")
plt.title("Violin Plot by Day")
plt.show()
```

### Explanation

Combines distribution shape with summary statistics.

------------------------------------------------------------------------

## Strip Plot

```{python}
sns.stripplot(data=tips, x="day", y="total_bill")
plt.title("Strip Plot")
plt.show()
```

### Explanation

Shows individual data points.

------------------------------------------------------------------------

## Swarm Plot

```{python}
sns.swarmplot(data=tips, x="day", y="total_bill")
plt.title("Swarm Plot")
plt.show()
```

### Explanation

Avoids overlapping points for clearer visualization.

------------------------------------------------------------------------

## Bar Plot

```{python}
sns.barplot(data=tips, x="day", y="total_bill")
plt.title("Average Total Bill by Day")
plt.show()
```

### Explanation

Shows average values with confidence intervals.

------------------------------------------------------------------------

## Count Plot

```{python}
sns.countplot(data=tips, x="day")
plt.title("Number of Records by Day")
plt.show()
```

### Explanation

Shows frequency of observations per category.

------------------------------------------------------------------------

# 3. Matrix Plots

Matrix plots show relationships using color encoding.

------------------------------------------------------------------------

## Correlation Matrix

```{python}
corr = tips.select_dtypes(include=np.number).corr()
corr.head()
```

------------------------------------------------------------------------

## Heatmap

```{python}
sns.heatmap(corr, annot=True, cmap="coolwarm")
plt.title("Correlation Heatmap")
plt.show()
```

------------------------------------------------------------------------

### 

# Conclusion

Seaborn is widely used in data science because it simplifies: - Distribution analysis\
- Category comparison\
- Relationship visualization\
- Correlation analysis
